children.name: parent_sample_id
children.description: The unique identifier from HuBMAP or SenNet for the sample (such as a block, section, or suspension) used to perform the assay. For instance, in an RNAseq assay, the parent sample would be the suspension, while in imaging assays, it would be the tissue section. If the assay is derived from multiple parent samples, this field should contain a comma-separated list of identifiers. Example: HBM386.ZGKG.235, HBM672.MKPK.442
children.label: Parent sample ID

children.name: lab_id
children.description: A locally assigned identifier provided by the data provider for the dataset. It is used to reference an external metadata record that may be maintained independently, enabling traceability and supporting provenance tracking. Example: Visium_9OLC_A4_S1
children.label: Lab ID

children.name: preparation_protocol_doi
children.description: The DOI for the protocols.io page that details the assay or the procedures used for sample procurement and preparation. For example, in the case of an imaging assay, the protocol may start with tissue section staining and end with the generation of an OME-TIFF file. The documented protocol should also include any image processing steps involved in producing the final OME-TIFF. Example: https://dx.doi.org/10.17504/protocols.io.eq2lyno9qvx9/v1
children.label: Preparation protocol DOI

children.name: dataset_type
children.description: The specific type of dataset being produced. Example: RNAseq
children.label: Dataset type

children.name: analyte_class
children.description: The analyte class which is the target molecule that the assay is measuring. Example: DNA
children.label: Analyte class

children.name: is_targeted
children.description: Indicates whether a specific molecule or set of molecules is targeted for detection or measurement by the assay. Example: Yes
children.label: Is targeted?

children.name: acquisition_instrument_vendor
children.description: The company that manufactures or supplies the acquisition instrument. An acquisition instrument is a device equipped with signal detection hardware and signal processing software. It captures signals produced by assays, such as variations in light intensity or color, or signals corresponding to molecular mass. If the instrument was custom-built or developed internally, enter "In-House". Example: Illumina
children.label: Acquisition instrument vendor

children.name: acquisition_instrument_model
children.description: The specific model of the acquisition instrument, as manufacturers often offer various versions with differing features or sensitivities. These differences may be relevant to the processing or interpretation of the data. If the instrument was custom-built or developed internally, enter "In-House". If the model is unknown, enter "Unknown". Example: HiSeq 4000
children.label: Acquisition instrument model

children.name: source_storage_duration_value
children.description: The length of time the sample was stored prior to processing it. For assays performed on tissue sections, this refers to how long the tissue section (e.g., slide) was stored before the assay began (e.g., imaging). For assays performed on suspensions, such as sequencing, it refers to how long the suspension was stored before library construction started. Example: 12
children.label: Source storage duration value

children.name: source_storage_duration_unit
children.description: The unit of measurement used to specify the source storage duration value. Example: hour
children.label: Source storage duration unit

children.name: time_since_acquisition_instrument_calibration_value
children.description: The length of time since the acquisition instrument was last serviced or calibrated. This provides a metric for assessing drift in data capture. Example: 10
children.label: Time since acquisition instrument calibration value

children.name: time_since_acquisition_instrument_calibration_unit
children.description: The unit of measurement used to specify the time since acquisition instrument calibration value. Example: month
children.label: Time since acquisition instrument calibration unit

default_value.label: Time since acquisition instrument calibration unit

children.name: contributors_path
children.description: The name of the file containing the ORCID IDs for all contributors to this dataset. Example: ./contributors.csv
children.label: Contributors path

children.name: data_path
children.description: The top-level directory containing the raw and/or processed data. For a single dataset upload, this might be represented as ".", whereas for a data upload containing multiple datasets, this would be the directory name for the respective dataset. For example, if the data is within a directory named "TEST001-RK", use the syntax "./TEST001-RK" for this field. If there are multiple directory levels, use the format "./TEST001-RK/Run1/Pass2", where "Pass2" is the subdirectory where the single dataset's data is stored. This is an internal metadata field used solely for data ingestion. Example: ./TEST001-RK
children.label: Data path

children.name: barcode_offset
children.description: The position within a sequencing read where a barcode sequence begins. In sequencing libraries, barcodes—short DNA sequences used to tag individual cells or molecules—are often arranged in a pattern where each barcode is separated by a constant sequence of known length. These constant sequences create predictable spacing between barcodes, known as offsets. For example, if barcodes are 8 base pairs (bp) long and separated by 30 bp constant regions, the barcodes might start at positions 0, 38, and 76 within the read. If the source material is not barcoded, this field should be marked as "Not applicable". Example: 0,38,76
children.label: Barcode offset

children.name: barcode_read
children.description: The sequencing read file contains the cell or capture spot barcode sequences. This information is essential when constructing sequencing libraries with a non-commercial kit, as it ensures that barcodes are correctly extracted during data processing. If the source material is not barcoded, this field should be marked as "Not applicable". Example: Read 1 (R1)
children.label: Barcode read

children.name: barcode_size
children.description: The length of each cell or capture spot barcode in base pairs (bp). If the source material is not barcoded, enter "Not applicable". Example: 16
children.label: Barcode size

children.name: umi_offset
children.description: The position within a sequencing read where the Unique Molecular Identifier (UMI) sequence begins. UMIs are short, random nucleotide sequences added to DNA or RNA fragments during library preparation to uniquely tag each original molecule. If no UMI is used, enter "Not applicable". Example: 16
children.label: UMI offset

children.name: umi_read
children.description: Specifies which sequencing read file contains the Unique Molecular Identifier (UMI) barcode. If no UMI is used, enter "Not applicable". Example: Read 1 (R1)
children.label: UMI read

children.name: umi_size
children.description: The length of the Unique Molecular Identifier (UMI) in base pairs (bp). If no UMI is used, enter "Not applicable". Example: 8
children.label: UMI size

children.name: assay_input_entity
children.description: The entity from which the analyte is captured. For example, in bulk sequencing, the source entity would be "tissue" while in single-cell sequencing, it would be "single cell". Example: single cell
children.label: Assay input entity

children.name: number_of_input_cells_or_nuclei
children.description: The number of cells or nuclei that were input into the assay. This information is typically not available for assays performed on bulk tissue preparations, and therefore this field may be left blank. Example: 1000
children.label: Number of input cells or nuclei

children.name: amount_of_input_analyte_value
children.description: The amount of RNA or DNA input into the assay, typically measured using instruments such as a Qubit, BioAnalyzer, or TapeStation. In most single-cell or single-nucleus assays, this value is usually not available, and therfore this field may be left blank. Example: 500
children.label: Amount of input analyte value

children.name: amount_of_input_analyte_unit
children.description: The unit of measurement used to specify the amount of input analyte value. If no input amount, this field may be left blank. Example: ng
children.label: Amount of input analyte unit

children.name: library_adapter_sequence
children.description: The 5' and/or 3' adapter sequences used during library preparation to make the library compatible with the sequencing protocol and instrumentation. Adapter sequences should be provided as a comma-separated list of key-value pairs in the format: "adapter name: sequence". Example: Nextera Read 1: CTGTCTCTTATACACATCT
children.label: Library adapter sequence

children.name: library_average_fragment_size
children.description: The average size of sequencing library fragments, measured in base pairs (bp), as estimated by gel electrophoresis, BioAnalyzer, or TapeStation. Example: 200
children.label: Library average fragment size

children.name: library_input_amount_value
children.description: The amount of cDNA, after amplification, that was used for library construction. Example: 60
children.label: Library input amount value

children.name: library_input_amount_unit
children.description: The unit of measurement used to specify the library input amount value. Example: ng
children.label: Library input amount unit

children.name: library_output_amount_value
children.description: The total amount of library following the clean-up step after the final PCR amplification. To calculate, multiply the Qubit-measured concentration (ng/µl) by the elution volume (µl) obtained after the final clean-up. Example: 125
children.label: Library output amount value

children.name: library_output_amount_unit
children.description: The unit of measurement used to specify the library output amount value. Example: ng
children.label: Library output amount unit

children.name: library_concentration_value
children.description: The concentration of the pooled library submitted for sequencing. Example: 2.5
children.label: Library concentration value

children.name: library_concentration_unit
children.description: Unit of library concentration value.
children.label: Library concentration unit

children.name: library_layout
children.description: Indicates whether the sequencing library was prepared for single-end or paired-end sequencing. This information determines how reads are generated and aligned during data processing. Example: single-end
children.label: Library layout

children.name: number_of_iterations_of_cdna_amplification
children.description: The number of amplification cycles applied to cDNA prior to library construction. This is typically the number of PCR cycles used. For protocols using linear amplification methods (e.g., aRNA), this refers to the number of amplification rounds performed. Example: 3
children.label: Number of iterations of cDNA amplification

children.name: number_of_pcr_cycles_for_indexing
children.description: The number of PCR cycles used specifically to add sequencing adapters and amplify the library during the indexing step. This count does not include earlier cDNA amplification cycles, which are recorded separately under the "Number of iterations of cDNA amplification" field. Example: 8
children.label: Number of PCR cycles for indexing

children.name: library_preparation_kit
children.description: The reagent kit used to construct the sequencing library, including both the kit name and, if available, version and product number. If the library was prepared using a custom protocol, enter “Custom”. Example: 10X Genomics; Chromium Next GEM Single Cell 5' Kit v2, 16 rxns; PN 1000263
children.label: Library preparation kit

children.name: sample_indexing_kit
children.description: The reagent kit used to add index sequences to samples. Indexes enable multiplexing, allowing multiple libraries to be pooled and sequenced together on the same Illumina flow cell. Each indexing kit includes a set of compatible index sequences (often called "indexing sets") used to uniquely label a specific number of samples, depending on the kit’s design. If the library was prepared using a custom protocol, enter “Custom”. If no indexing was performed, enter "Not applicable". Example: 10X Genomics; Dual Index Kit TS, Set A; PN 1000251
children.label: Sample indexing kit

children.name: sample_indexing_set
children.description: The specific sequencing barcode index set used, chosen from the sample indexing kit. For example, in the case of 10x, this might be "SI-GA-A1", whereas for Nextera, it could be "N505 - CTCCTTAC". Example: SI-GA-A1
children.label: Sample indexing set

children.name: is_technical_replicate
children.description: Indicates whether the sequencing reaction was run in replicate. If "Yes," the corresponding FASTQ files in the dataset should be merged for analysis. Example: Yes
children.label: Is technical replicate?

children.name: expected_entity_capture_count
children.description: The number of cells, nuclei, or capture spots expected to be captured by the assay. Example: 14336
children.label: Expected entity capture count

children.name: sequencing_reagent_kit
children.description: The reagent kit used to perform the sequencing run, including the platform, kit name, version, and product number when available. If the reagent kit was prepared using a custom protocol, enter “Custom”. Example: Illumina; NextSeq 500/550 Hi Output Kit 150 Cycles; v2.5; PN 20024907
children.label: Sequencing reagent kit

children.name: sequencing_read_format
children.description: The number of sequencing cycles for each read and index in the sequencing run, typically reported as a comma-separated list in the order: Read 1, i7 index, i5 index, and Read 2. Example: 50,8,16,50
children.label: Sequencing read format

children.name: sequencing_batch_id
children.description: A lab-assigned identifier for the sequencing run, such as a chip ID, that enables users to determine which samples were processed together. To avoid ID conflicts across institutions, it is recommended to prefix the ID with the name of the sequencing center. Example: Broad_Chip1234
children.label: Sequencing batch ID

children.name: capture_batch_id
children.description: A lab-assigned identifier used to indicate which cells or nuclei were captured together in the same run. For example, in a 10X Genomics Chromium Controller workflow, this could be the chip ID used to trace datasets derived from a single capture event. This ID helps users identify samples processed together on the same device. To avoid conflicts across institutions, it is recommended to prefix the ID with the name of the sequencing center. Example: Broad_Batch1234
children.label: Capture batch ID

children.name: preparation_instrument_vendor
children.description: The company that manufactures the instrument used to prepare the sample (e.g., for staining or other processing steps) prior to the assay. If the instrument was custom-built or developed internally, enter "In-House". If no sample preparation occurred, enter "Not applicable". Example: 10X Genomics
children.label: Preparation instrument vendor

children.name: preparation_instrument_model
children.description: The specific model of the instrument used for sample preparation, such as staining. Manufacturers may offer multiple models with varying features or sensitivities, which can influence how the sample is processed and how the resulting data is interpreted. If no sample preparation occurred, enter "Not applicable". Example: Chromium X
children.label: Preparation instrument model

children.name: preparation_instrument_kit
children.description: The reagent kit used in conjunction with the preparation instrument for sample processing (e.g., staining or labeling). If the reagent kit was prepared using a custom protocol, enter “Custom”. Example: 10X Genomics; Chromium Next GEM Chip G Single Cell Kit, 48 rxns; PN 1000120
children.label: Preparation instrument kit

children.name: metadata_schema_id
children.description: The unique string identifier for the metadata specification version, which is easily interpretable by computers for purposes of data validation and processing. Example: 22bc762a-5020-419d-b170-24253ed9e8d9
children.label: Metadata schema ID
